name: NIST Cybersecurity Framework 2.0
description: The NIST Cybersecurity Framework (CSF) 2.0 provides a taxonomy of high-level cybersecurity outcomes that can be used by any organization — regardless of size, sector, or maturity — to better understand, assess, prioritize, and communicate cybersecurity efforts. CSF 2.0 introduces the GOVERN function and expands supply chain risk management guidance.

functions.name: GOVERN
functions.description: The organization's cybersecurity risk management strategy, expectations, and policy are established, communicated, and monitored.

categories.name: Organizational Context
categories.description: The circumstances — mission, stakeholder expectations, dependencies, and legal/regulatory requirements — surrounding the organization's cybersecurity risk decisions are understood.

controls.name: Organizational mission and cybersecurity alignment
controls.question: Has the organization documented its mission, strategic objectives, and key stakeholders, and are cybersecurity risk decisions aligned with those priorities?
controls.evidence_guidance: Request: Mission statement, strategic plan, board meeting minutes referencing cybersecurity, organizational chart, stakeholder register.
controls.why_it_matters: Without understanding organizational context, cybersecurity investments may be misaligned with business priorities, resulting in gaps where it matters most or overspending where it does not.
common_weaknesses: Security program not tied to business strategy
No documented mission or business objectives
IT and security not represented in strategic planning
controls.remediation_guidance: Facilitate a meeting between security leadership and executive management to document business objectives and map cybersecurity priorities accordingly. Produce a one-page alignment document reviewed annually.
controls.risk_impact: Misaligned security investment; critical business risks left unaddressed; security treated as an IT cost rather than a business enabler.

scoring_guidance.4: Reviewed and updated annually with executive sign-off

controls.name: Legal and regulatory requirements understood
controls.question: Has the organization identified all applicable cybersecurity-related legal, regulatory, and contractual obligations (e.g., HIPAA, PCI DSS, CMMC, state breach notification laws)?
controls.evidence_guidance: Request: Compliance obligation register, contracts with security requirements, legal counsel opinion on applicable regulations, evidence of compliance reviews.
controls.why_it_matters: Non-compliance with legal and regulatory requirements can result in fines, litigation, loss of contracts, and reputational damage.
common_weaknesses: No formal compliance inventory
Reliance on outdated understanding of requirements
Contractual security requirements not flowed down to vendors
controls.remediation_guidance: Develop a compliance obligations register. Assign ownership for each applicable regulation. Review annually or when entering new markets or signing new contracts.
controls.risk_impact: Regulatory fines, contract termination, data breach liability, reputational harm.

scoring_guidance.4: Register maintained and reviewed; compliance verified

controls.name: Critical outcomes and business services identified
controls.question: Has the organization identified its critical business processes, products, and services whose disruption would have the most significant impact?
controls.evidence_guidance: Request: Business impact analysis (BIA), critical asset list, service catalog, revenue-generating process documentation.
controls.why_it_matters: Without knowing what matters most, organizations cannot prioritize cybersecurity investments effectively or plan meaningful continuity strategies.
common_weaknesses: No formal BIA exists
IT and business define 'critical' differently
Critical services identified but not reviewed after organizational changes
controls.remediation_guidance: Conduct a formal Business Impact Analysis. Identify recovery time objectives (RTOs) and recovery point objectives (RPOs) for critical services. Review after major organizational changes.
controls.risk_impact: Inability to prioritize incident response, prolonged outages of critical services, inability to meet continuity objectives.

scoring_guidance.4: BIA maintained, tested, and reviewed annually

categories.name: Risk Management Strategy
categories.description: The organization's priorities, constraints, risk tolerance and appetite statements, and assumptions are established, communicated, and used to support operational risk decisions.

controls.name: Risk management policy established
controls.question: Does the organization have a documented cybersecurity risk management policy that defines risk appetite, tolerance thresholds, and the overall risk management approach?
controls.evidence_guidance: Request: Risk management policy, risk appetite statement, board/executive approval documentation, risk register.
controls.why_it_matters: Without a defined risk appetite, security decisions are inconsistent and may not reflect the organization's actual willingness to accept risk.
common_weaknesses: No formal risk policy
Risk appetite undefined or not communicated to staff
Risk decisions made ad hoc without consistent criteria
controls.remediation_guidance: Draft a risk management policy defining risk appetite (e.g., acceptable risk score thresholds), risk treatment options, and roles/responsibilities. Obtain executive approval and review annually.
controls.risk_impact: Inconsistent risk decisions, over- or under-investment in security, inability to communicate risk posture to leadership.

scoring_guidance.4: Policy reviewed annually with documented risk appetite

controls.name: Risk management roles and responsibilities
controls.question: Are roles and responsibilities for cybersecurity risk management clearly defined, assigned, and understood across the organization?
controls.evidence_guidance: Request: RACI matrix, job descriptions mentioning security responsibilities, security committee charter, evidence of security role assignments.
controls.why_it_matters: When nobody owns risk decisions, critical risks go unaddressed. Clear accountability ensures risks are identified, tracked, and treated.
common_weaknesses: Security responsibility assumed to be IT-only
No named CISO or security lead
Board not informed of significant cybersecurity risks
controls.remediation_guidance: Define a RACI model for cybersecurity risk management. Designate a security lead (CISO, security manager, or vCISO). Establish a governance committee with executive membership.
controls.risk_impact: Unowned risks, slow incident response, inability to make coordinated risk decisions.

scoring_guidance.4: Roles formalized with performance metrics

categories.name: Policies, Processes, and Procedures
categories.description: Organizational cybersecurity policies and procedures are established, communicated, and enforced.

controls.name: Cybersecurity policy suite exists
controls.question: Does the organization maintain an up-to-date suite of cybersecurity policies covering at minimum: acceptable use, access control, password management, incident response, data classification, and vendor management?
controls.evidence_guidance: Request: Policy library or index, individual policy documents, policy review dates and approvals, evidence of employee acknowledgment.
controls.why_it_matters: Policies establish the rules of the road for cybersecurity. Without them, employees have no clear guidance, and auditors have no baseline to measure against.
common_weaknesses: Policies exist but are outdated (> 2 years old)
Policies not reviewed after significant changes
Staff unaware policies exist
No employee acknowledgment process
controls.remediation_guidance: Develop a policy library covering the core cybersecurity domains. Establish an annual review cycle. Require employee acknowledgment via HR or LMS system.
controls.risk_impact: Non-compliance, inconsistent security practices, regulatory violations, inability to hold employees accountable for security incidents.

scoring_guidance.4: Comprehensive policy suite, acknowledged by all staff, reviewed and updated

controls.name: Cybersecurity roles in HR processes
controls.question: Are cybersecurity requirements integrated into HR processes including hiring background checks, onboarding training, role changes, and offboarding access revocation?
controls.evidence_guidance: Request: HR onboarding checklist, offboarding checklist showing access revocation steps, background check policy, security awareness training completion records.
controls.why_it_matters: The majority of data breaches involve an insider element. Integrating security into HR processes reduces the risk of malicious or negligent insider threats.
common_weaknesses: Access not revoked promptly upon termination
No background checks for sensitive roles
Security training not included in onboarding
Role change access reviews not performed
controls.remediation_guidance: Integrate security checkpoints into all HR lifecycle processes. Implement automated offboarding triggers that flag IT for immediate access revocation. Require security training within 30 days of hire.
controls.risk_impact: Unauthorized access by former employees, malicious insider risk, regulatory violations, data exposure.

scoring_guidance.4: Fully automated with audit trails

functions.name: IDENTIFY
functions.description: The organization's current cybersecurity risks are understood.

categories.name: Asset Management
categories.description: Assets (data, hardware, software, systems, and facilities) that enable the organization to achieve business purposes are identified and managed consistent with their relative importance to organizational objectives and risk strategy.

controls.name: Hardware asset inventory
controls.question: Does the organization maintain an accurate and current inventory of all hardware assets (workstations, servers, network devices, mobile devices, IoT) authorized to connect to the network?
controls.evidence_guidance: Request: Asset inventory spreadsheet or CMDB, network discovery scan results, MDM enrollment list, procurement records.
controls.why_it_matters: You cannot protect what you don't know exists. Unmanaged devices are common entry points for attackers.
common_weaknesses: Inventory is manual and inaccurate
Shadow IT devices not included
No process to add/remove devices from inventory
IoT/OT devices excluded
controls.remediation_guidance: Deploy a network discovery tool (e.g., Nmap, Lansweeper, or an agent-based solution). Establish a process to update the inventory when devices are provisioned or decommissioned.
controls.risk_impact: Unpatched, unmanaged devices become attack vectors; inability to respond to incidents affecting unknown assets.

scoring_guidance.4: Real-time inventory with automated reconciliation and alerting for unauthorized devices

controls.name: Software asset inventory
controls.question: Does the organization maintain an inventory of authorized software applications, including versions, vendors, and end-of-life status?
controls.evidence_guidance: Request: Software license inventory, approved software list, patch management reports showing installed software, MDM/EDR software inventory.
controls.why_it_matters: Unauthorized software introduces vulnerabilities and licensing risk. Untracked software cannot be patched or monitored for exploits.
common_weaknesses: No approved software list
Unauthorized tools installed by employees
End-of-life software still in use
No process to track SaaS subscriptions
controls.remediation_guidance: Implement application whitelisting or maintain an approved software list. Use endpoint management tools to audit installed software. Flag unsupported/EOL software for removal or compensating controls.
controls.risk_impact: Unpatched vulnerabilities, malware from unauthorized software, shadow IT data exposure.

scoring_guidance.4: Automated inventory with EOL monitoring and unauthorized software alerting

controls.name: Data classification and sensitivity labeling
controls.question: Has the organization implemented a data classification scheme that categorizes data by sensitivity (e.g., Public, Internal, Confidential, Restricted) and applied appropriate handling requirements to each tier?
controls.evidence_guidance: Request: Data classification policy, examples of labeled documents/emails, data flow diagrams, data inventory by classification tier.
controls.why_it_matters: Without knowing what data is sensitive and where it lives, organizations cannot apply proportionate security controls, leading to both over- and under-protection.
common_weaknesses: No classification scheme defined
Classification defined but not applied in practice
Sensitive data discovered in unexpected locations (e.g., email, shared drives)
controls.remediation_guidance: Define a data classification scheme with 3–4 tiers. Train employees on how to classify and handle each tier. Implement technical controls (DLP, labeling tools) to enforce classification.
controls.risk_impact: Sensitive data exposed in inappropriate locations, inability to respond proportionately to data breaches, regulatory non-compliance.

scoring_guidance.4: Technical enforcement via DLP and labeling tools

categories.name: Risk Assessment
categories.description: The cybersecurity risk to the organization, assets, and individuals is understood by the organization.

controls.name: Vulnerability identification process
controls.question: Does the organization conduct regular vulnerability assessments or scans of IT systems to identify security weaknesses?
controls.evidence_guidance: Request: Vulnerability scan reports (last 3–6 months), scan schedule, scan tool documentation, evidence of remediation tracking.
controls.why_it_matters: Unidentified vulnerabilities are routinely exploited by attackers. Regular scanning catches known weaknesses before threat actors do.
common_weaknesses: No scanning performed
Scans infrequent (< quarterly)
Scans performed but results not acted upon
Scan scope excludes key systems
controls.remediation_guidance: Deploy a vulnerability scanner (e.g., Tenable Nessus, Qualys, Rapid7). Scan all in-scope assets at least quarterly. Track and remediate findings by severity within defined SLAs.
controls.risk_impact: Known vulnerabilities exploited; delayed patch cycles; compliance failure (PCI DSS, HIPAA require regular scanning).

scoring_guidance.4: Continuous scanning with SLA-based remediation

controls.name: Threat intelligence integration
controls.question: Does the organization subscribe to and act on relevant threat intelligence sources (e.g., CISA advisories, ISACs, vendor security bulletins) to understand emerging threats?
controls.evidence_guidance: Request: Threat intelligence subscriptions, evidence of threat alerts being reviewed, patching triggered by threat intel, incident response triggered by threat intel.
controls.why_it_matters: Threat intelligence provides advance warning of emerging attack techniques, enabling proactive defenses rather than reactive response.
common_weaknesses: No threat intelligence subscriptions
Alerts received but not acted on
Intelligence not contextualized for the organization's environment
controls.remediation_guidance: Subscribe to CISA alerts, vendor security bulletins, and an industry ISAC (if applicable). Assign someone to review and triage alerts weekly. Establish a process to accelerate patching when active exploitation is confirmed.
controls.risk_impact: Late awareness of active attacks; known exploits used against your organization while patches were available.

scoring_guidance.4: Structured threat intel program with contextualization and action tracking

functions.name: PROTECT
functions.description: Safeguards to manage the organization's cybersecurity risks are used.

categories.name: Identity Management, Authentication, and Access Control
categories.description: Access to physical and logical assets is limited to authorized users, services, and hardware and managed commensurate with the assessed risk.

controls.name: Identity and credential management
controls.question: Does the organization manage identities and credentials for all users, services, and systems including provisioning, periodic review, and timely deprovisioning?
controls.evidence_guidance: Request: User account audit report, access provisioning procedures, access review records (quarterly or semi-annual), deprovisioning logs, Active Directory or IdP export.
controls.why_it_matters: Orphaned accounts, stale credentials, and over-privileged users are among the most commonly exploited attack vectors.
common_weaknesses: Terminated employee accounts not disabled promptly
No periodic access review
Shared/generic accounts in use
Service accounts with excessive privilege
controls.remediation_guidance: Implement an identity lifecycle management process. Conduct access reviews at least semi-annually. Disable accounts within 24 hours of termination. Eliminate shared accounts.
controls.risk_impact: Unauthorized access, credential theft, insider threat exploitation of orphaned accounts.

scoring_guidance.4: Automated lifecycle management with real-time deprovisioning

controls.name: Multi-factor authentication (MFA)
controls.question: Is multi-factor authentication (MFA) required for all remote access, cloud services, privileged accounts, and email — and is it enforced technically rather than by policy alone?
controls.evidence_guidance: Request: MFA configuration screenshots, Conditional Access or similar policy exports, VPN configuration, MFA enrollment reports, exceptions list with approval.
controls.why_it_matters: MFA is the single most effective control against credential theft and account takeover. The vast majority of breaches involving stolen credentials would have been prevented by MFA.
common_weaknesses: MFA enabled but not enforced (user can bypass)
MFA not required for privileged accounts
Legacy authentication protocols (SMTP AUTH, basic auth) bypass MFA
SMS-only MFA for high-risk accounts
controls.remediation_guidance: Enforce MFA via Conditional Access (Azure AD) or equivalent for all users. Prioritize privileged accounts and remote access. Block legacy authentication protocols. Consider phishing-resistant MFA (FIDO2/hardware keys) for admin accounts.
controls.risk_impact: Account takeover, business email compromise, ransomware deployment via stolen credentials.

scoring_guidance.3: MFA enforced for all remote access and privileged accounts
scoring_guidance.4: MFA enforced for all users with phishing-resistant MFA for privileged accounts; legacy auth blocked

controls.name: Least privilege and need-to-know
controls.question: Are user access rights granted based on least privilege and business need-to-know, and are privileged rights managed separately from standard user accounts?
controls.evidence_guidance: Request: Role-based access control documentation, privileged access management (PAM) tool evidence, admin account audit, evidence of access request/approval workflow.
controls.why_it_matters: Over-privileged accounts dramatically increase the blast radius of any account compromise. Least privilege limits lateral movement and data access if an account is breached.
common_weaknesses: Domain admin accounts used for daily tasks
No separation of admin and user accounts
Access accumulated over time without periodic review
No PAM solution for privileged accounts
controls.remediation_guidance: Implement RBAC. Require all administrators to use separate privileged accounts for admin tasks. Deploy a PAM solution for privileged credential management. Conduct quarterly privilege reviews.
controls.risk_impact: Lateral movement by attackers, widespread data access from a single compromised account, ransomware spreading with admin rights.

scoring_guidance.2: Admin accounts exist but PAM not used
scoring_guidance.3: RBAC implemented with separate admin accounts
scoring_guidance.4: PAM solution in use; just-in-time access for privileged operations

categories.name: Data Security
categories.description: Data are managed consistent with the organization's risk strategy to protect the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information.

controls.name: Data-at-rest encryption
controls.question: Is sensitive data encrypted at rest on all endpoints, servers, and storage systems, including portable media?
controls.evidence_guidance: Request: Endpoint encryption policy, BitLocker or FileVault deployment reports, server/database encryption configuration, portable media encryption policy and evidence.
controls.why_it_matters: Encryption at rest renders stolen data unreadable, protecting against physical theft of devices and storage media.
common_weaknesses: Full-disk encryption not deployed on laptops
Portable media unencrypted
Database encryption not enabled
Keys poorly managed or stored alongside data
controls.remediation_guidance: Enable BitLocker (Windows) or FileVault (Mac) on all endpoints. Enforce removable media encryption. Enable TDE on databases containing sensitive data. Implement proper key management.
controls.risk_impact: Loss or theft of unencrypted devices exposes sensitive data; regulatory fines (HIPAA breach safe harbor requires encryption).

scoring_guidance.3: All endpoints and key servers encrypted
scoring_guidance.4: All data-at-rest encrypted including databases; key management solution in use

controls.name: Data-in-transit encryption
controls.question: Is all sensitive data encrypted in transit using current TLS standards, and are insecure protocols (Telnet, FTP, HTTP, SNMPv1/v2) disabled or restricted?
controls.evidence_guidance: Request: Network scan results showing open ports/protocols, TLS configuration assessment, web application SSL labs results, VPN configuration, internal traffic encryption evidence.
controls.why_it_matters: Unencrypted data in transit is trivially intercepted on the network, exposing credentials, PII, and business data.
common_weaknesses: HTTP used instead of HTTPS for internal applications
Outdated TLS versions (TLS 1.0/1.1) still enabled
Telnet/FTP still in use
Email transmitted without TLS enforcement
controls.remediation_guidance: Enforce HTTPS/TLS 1.2+ on all web applications. Disable Telnet, FTP, SNMPv1/v2, and other insecure protocols. Enforce STARTTLS or SMTP over TLS for email. Scan for weak cipher suites.
controls.risk_impact: Man-in-the-middle attacks, credential interception, sensitive data exposure on the wire.

scoring_guidance.3: TLS enforced for all external traffic; insecure protocols disabled
scoring_guidance.4: TLS 1.2+ enforced everywhere; internal traffic also encrypted; cipher suites hardened

categories.name: Platform Security
categories.description: The hardware, software, and services of physical and virtual platforms are managed consistent with the organization's risk strategy.

controls.name: Security configuration and hardening
controls.question: Are systems configured securely using industry-standard hardening benchmarks (e.g., CIS Benchmarks, DISA STIGs), with unnecessary services disabled and default credentials changed?
controls.evidence_guidance: Request: Configuration management policy, CIS benchmark scan results, baseline configuration templates, evidence of hardening on servers/workstations/network devices.
controls.why_it_matters: Default and misconfigured systems are among the most exploited targets. Hardening removes unnecessary attack surface.
common_weaknesses: Default credentials not changed on network equipment
Unnecessary services running on servers
No documented baseline configuration
Cloud services deployed with default (insecure) settings
controls.remediation_guidance: Adopt CIS Benchmarks as configuration baselines. Use configuration management tools to enforce baselines. Scan for drift from approved configurations regularly.
controls.risk_impact: Default credential exploitation, unauthorized access via unnecessary services, misconfigured cloud services exposing data.

scoring_guidance.3: CIS Benchmarks applied to key systems
scoring_guidance.4: Automated baseline enforcement and drift detection across all systems

controls.name: Patch and vulnerability management
controls.question: Does the organization have a documented patch management process with defined timelines for applying critical, high, and routine patches across all systems?
controls.evidence_guidance: Request: Patch management policy, patch deployment reports (last 30/60/90 days), evidence of critical patch application timelines, exception process.
controls.why_it_matters: The vast majority of exploits target known, patchable vulnerabilities. Timely patching is the most effective technical control to prevent exploitation.
common_weaknesses: No defined patching SLAs
Patching infrequent (> 30 days for critical)
Critical systems excluded from patch management
End-of-life systems that cannot be patched still in production
controls.remediation_guidance: Establish patch SLAs: Critical (72 hours), High (14 days), Medium (30 days), Low (90 days). Deploy a patch management platform. Track compliance against SLAs. Compensating controls for systems that cannot be patched.
controls.risk_impact: Known vulnerability exploitation, ransomware deployment via unpatched systems, compliance failure.

scoring_guidance.4: Automated patch deployment with SLA compliance reporting and exception management

categories.name: Awareness and Training
categories.description: The organization's personnel are provided with cybersecurity awareness and training so that they can perform their cybersecurity-related tasks.

controls.name: Security awareness training
controls.question: Does the organization provide security awareness training to all employees at least annually, covering topics such as phishing, password hygiene, social engineering, and data handling?
controls.evidence_guidance: Request: Training completion records, training platform screenshots, training curriculum/syllabus, phishing simulation results.
controls.why_it_matters: Employees are the most commonly exploited attack vector. Training measurably reduces susceptibility to phishing and social engineering.
common_weaknesses: Training conducted only at onboarding
Generic training not relevant to the organization
Completion rates below 90%
No phishing simulations
controls.remediation_guidance: Implement annual security awareness training for all employees. Add role-specific training for high-risk roles (finance, executives, IT). Run quarterly phishing simulations with remedial training for clickers.
controls.risk_impact: Phishing susceptibility, BEC attacks, accidental data disclosure, malware infection from email attachments.

scoring_guidance.3: Annual training with > 90% completion and phishing simulations
scoring_guidance.4: Continuous training, role-specific modules, phishing simulations, metrics-driven improvement

functions.name: DETECT
functions.description: Possible cybersecurity attacks and compromises are found and analyzed.

categories.name: Continuous Monitoring
categories.description: Assets are monitored to find anomalies, indicators of compromise, and other potentially adverse events.

controls.name: Network monitoring and anomaly detection
controls.question: Is network traffic monitored for anomalies, unauthorized connections, and indicators of compromise, and are alerts reviewed in a timely manner?
controls.evidence_guidance: Request: SIEM or network monitoring tool evidence, alert review logs, IDS/IPS configuration, network traffic reports, firewall log review evidence.
controls.why_it_matters: Most breaches go undetected for weeks or months. Network monitoring shortens the dwell time of attackers and enables faster containment.
common_weaknesses: Firewall logs not reviewed
No SIEM or centralized logging
Alerts generated but not reviewed
No baseline of normal network behavior established
controls.remediation_guidance: Implement a SIEM or at minimum centralized log management. Configure alerts for high-priority events. Assign responsibility for daily alert review. Establish network baselines to detect anomalous traffic.
controls.risk_impact: Late detection of breaches, extended attacker dwell time, inability to determine scope of incidents.

scoring_guidance.3: SIEM with automated alerting and regular review
scoring_guidance.4: 24/7 monitoring with SOC or MSSP; automated detection and response

controls.name: Endpoint detection and response (EDR)
controls.question: Are endpoint detection and response (EDR) or advanced antivirus/anti-malware solutions deployed on all endpoints and servers, with centralized management and monitoring?
controls.evidence_guidance: Request: EDR/AV deployment report, coverage percentage, recent alert/detection logs, centralized console screenshots, update status report.
controls.why_it_matters: Traditional signature-based antivirus misses modern malware and fileless attacks. EDR provides behavioral detection and forensic capability needed to detect and respond to advanced threats.
common_weaknesses: Legacy AV only — no behavioral detection
Gaps in coverage (some endpoints unmanaged)
EDR deployed but alerts not reviewed
Outdated definitions
controls.remediation_guidance: Deploy a next-gen EDR solution (CrowdStrike, SentinelOne, Microsoft Defender for Endpoint) on all endpoints and servers. Centrally manage and monitor detections. Ensure definitions and agent versions are current.
controls.risk_impact: Undetected malware, ransomware deployment, inability to perform forensic investigation post-incident.

scoring_guidance.3: EDR on all endpoints with centralized management
scoring_guidance.4: EDR with managed detection and response (MDR); behavioral analytics in use

categories.name: Adverse Event Analysis
categories.description: Anomalies, indicators of compromise, and other potentially adverse events are analyzed to characterize the events and detect cybersecurity incidents.

controls.name: Security event correlation and analysis
controls.question: Does the organization have a defined process to analyze, correlate, and investigate security events to determine whether an incident has occurred?
controls.evidence_guidance: Request: SIEM correlation rule configuration, documented triage procedures, incident log entries showing event investigation, SOC playbooks.
controls.why_it_matters: Individual security alerts are often noise. Correlation and analysis of multiple events reveals patterns consistent with actual attacks.
common_weaknesses: No correlation rules in SIEM
Individual alerts reviewed in isolation
No documented triage process
Alert fatigue due to high volume of untuned alerts
controls.remediation_guidance: Develop correlation rules and use cases relevant to your environment. Create documented triage playbooks. Tune alerts to reduce false positives. Assign dedicated analyst time for investigation.
controls.risk_impact: Missed indicators of attack, inability to detect multi-stage attacks, slow incident response.

scoring_guidance.3: Documented process with correlation and regular review
scoring_guidance.4: Mature detection engineering with tuned use cases and metrics

functions.name: RESPOND
functions.description: Actions regarding a detected cybersecurity incident are taken.

categories.name: Incident Management
categories.description: Responses to detected cybersecurity incidents are managed.

controls.name: Incident response plan
controls.question: Does the organization have a documented, tested incident response plan that defines roles, escalation procedures, external notification requirements, and containment/eradication steps?
controls.evidence_guidance: Request: Incident response plan document, most recent IRP test/tabletop exercise record, contact list for incident response team, evidence of IRP update after incidents.
controls.why_it_matters: Without a plan, organizations improvise during high-stress incidents, leading to errors, delays, and failure to meet legal notification requirements.
common_weaknesses: No written IRP
IRP exists but never tested
Contact lists outdated
Notification requirements not included
Roles not pre-assigned
controls.remediation_guidance: Develop an IRP aligned with NIST SP 800-61. Define the incident response team, escalation paths, and external contacts (legal, insurer, breach counsel, law enforcement). Test via annual tabletop exercise.
controls.risk_impact: Slow, disorganized incident response; missed legal notification deadlines; extended downtime; regulatory penalties.

scoring_guidance.3: Written IRP with annual tabletop exercise
scoring_guidance.4: IRP tested annually; lessons learned incorporated; notification procedures verified

controls.name: Incident classification and escalation
controls.question: Does the organization use a defined severity classification scheme for incidents, with documented escalation procedures and decision criteria for involving executive management and legal counsel?
controls.evidence_guidance: Request: Incident severity matrix, escalation procedures, evidence of escalation in recent incidents, executive communication templates.
controls.why_it_matters: Improper escalation — especially failing to notify executives or legal counsel of significant incidents — can result in missed notification deadlines and compounded legal exposure.
common_weaknesses: All incidents treated the same regardless of severity
No criteria for escalating to legal or executives
PR/communications team not included in escalation plan
controls.remediation_guidance: Define a severity matrix (P1–P4) with explicit escalation thresholds. Document who to call at each severity level, including legal counsel, cyber insurance carrier, and executives.
controls.risk_impact: Under-response to serious incidents, missed regulatory notification deadlines, legal liability.

scoring_guidance.3: Severity matrix with documented escalation paths
scoring_guidance.4: Tested escalation procedures with defined SLAs for each severity level

functions.name: RECOVER
functions.description: Assets and operations affected by a cybersecurity incident are restored.

categories.name: Incident Recovery Plan Execution
categories.description: Restoration activities are performed to ensure operational availability of systems and services affected by cybersecurity incidents.

controls.name: Recovery plan exists and is tested
controls.question: Does the organization have a documented recovery plan that includes steps to restore systems and data following a cybersecurity incident, and has this plan been tested?
controls.evidence_guidance: Request: Business continuity plan, disaster recovery plan, most recent recovery test results, backup restoration test records.
controls.why_it_matters: Organizations that discover their recovery plan does not work during an active incident face catastrophic consequences. Testing reveals gaps before they matter.
common_weaknesses: No recovery plan beyond informal IT restoration
Backup recovery not tested
Recovery plan not updated after infrastructure changes
Recovery time objectives not defined
controls.remediation_guidance: Develop a recovery plan covering all critical systems with defined RTOs and RPOs. Test backup restoration quarterly. Conduct a full recovery test annually. Update the plan after major infrastructure changes.
controls.risk_impact: Extended downtime, data loss, business failure following a ransomware or major incident.

scoring_guidance.4: Comprehensive BCP/DRP with quarterly backup tests and annual full-scale exercise

categories.name: Incident Recovery Communication
categories.description: Restoration activities are coordinated with internal and external parties.

controls.name: Recovery communications
controls.question: Does the organization have documented communication procedures for notifying stakeholders, regulators, customers, and the public following a cybersecurity incident?
controls.evidence_guidance: Request: Communication plan, notification templates, regulatory notification procedures, PR/communications team integration in IRP.
controls.why_it_matters: Poor communication during or after an incident amplifies reputational damage and may violate legal notification requirements.
common_weaknesses: No communication plan
Legal/PR not involved until late in incident response
Notification templates not prepared in advance
controls.remediation_guidance: Develop communication templates for regulatory notifications, customer notifications, and press statements. Pre-designate spokespeople. Include communications team in IRP. Review applicable notification timelines (e.g., GDPR 72-hour, state breach laws).
controls.risk_impact: Regulatory penalties for late notification, reputational damage from poor communication, loss of customer trust.

scoring_guidance.3: Documented plan with templates and approved spokespeople
scoring_guidance.4: Tested communication plan with legal-verified notification timelines
